title: AlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework. A Rigorous IS WFA OOS Protocol for Mitigating Overfitting in Quantitative Strategies
summary: Transitioning a strategy from backtest to live trading is a common failure point for quantitative systems due to parameter overfitting, selection bias, and sensitivity to regime changes. This paper presents the AlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework, a standardized protocol that evaluates strategies across three stages: In Sample (IS), which focuses on stable parameter regions instead of single optima; Walk Forward Analysis (WFA) using rolling windows and purge gaps to reduce information leakage, supported by majority pass and catastrophic veto rules; and Out of Sample (OOS) testing under strict parameter lock with no further tuning. The framework applies a defense in depth structure that includes structural safeguards such as cliff veto, execution controls such as spread and leverage guards, and equity protection mechanisms such as circuit breakers and a kill switch. A case study on USDJPY M5 intraday data demonstrates how to detect overfitting through performance decay and drawdown behavior across chronological stages. A post validation comparison of four alpha variants (v1 to v4) shows rank reversal when the objective changes from maximizing Sharpe to minimizing maximum drawdown, highlighting the trade off between risk adjusted performance and tail risk control.
text:
AlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework: A Rigorous IS–WF A–OOS
Protocol for Mitigating Overfitting in Quantitative Strategies
Nguyen Thi Nguyet1,4, Nguyen Bao Chan2,4, and Pham The Anh∗3,4
1ds.nguyetnt@gmail.com
2nguyenbaochan20042002@gmail.com
3pta.forwork@gmail.com
4AlgoXpert Lab – Algorithmic Trading, Quantitative Finance, AI & Data Science,
https://algoxpert.org/,
Corresponding author:admin@algoxpert.org
Abstract
Transitioning a strategy from backtest to live operation is where many quantitative
trading systems fail, primarily due to parameter overfitting, selection bias, and fragility under
regime shifts. This paper proposes theAlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework—a
standardized, decision-oriented protocol that evaluates strategies across three chronological
stages: (i) In-Sample (IS), which prioritizes stable parameter regions (plateaus) over single
optima; (ii) Walk-Forward Analysis (WF A) with rolling windows and purge gaps to mitigate
information leakage, equipped with majority-pass and catastrophic-veto decision gates;
and (iii) Out-of-Sample (OOS) holdout under strict parameter lock (no further tuning).
The framework integrates a defense-in-depth architecture: structural (cliff veto), execution
(spread/leverage guards), and equity protection (circuit breakers, kill switch). A case
study on USDJPY M5 intraday illustrates the procedure for detecting overfitting through
performance degradation and drawdown integrity under chronology. A post-validation report
comparing four alpha variants (v1–v4) reveals rank reversal when switching the objective from
maximizing Sharpe to minimizing MaxDD, underscoring the trade-off between risk-adjusted
performance and tail-risk control.
Keywords:Alpha Research Framework; Walk-Forward Analysis; Purged Validation; Pa-
rameter Stability; Backtest Overfitting; Selection Bias; Execution-Aware Backtesting; Stress
Testing; Kill Switch; Out-of-Sample Verification.
Disclaimer.For research purposes only; not investment advice. Past performance does not
guarantee future results.
1 Introduction
In algorithmic trading, the gap between a seemingly compelling backtest alpha and a stable
live system is where strategies most often fail. Three prevalent causes are: (i) excessive
parameter optimization on finite data, causing the model to fit noise (overfitting) [ 4], (ii) testing
numerous configurations or variants without proper controls, leading to selection bias [ 5, 6],
and (iii) time-varying changes in volatility, liquidity, and execution costs that render strategies
fragile under regime shifts. Accordingly, the objective of this paper is not to maximize “peak
backtest” performance, but to standardize adeployment decision protocolthat is reproducible
and auditable: a strategy advances only upon passing clearly defined decision gates in strict
chronological order.
∗Founder & CEO, AlgoXpert
1
arXiv:2603.09219v1  [q-fin.PM]  10 Mar 2026
1.1 Practical Gaps
In practice, many useful techniques exist—walk-forward analysis [ 1], parameter stability checks,
stress testing, and data-leakage mitigation [ 3]. However, these are often applied in isolation,
lacking an end-to-endprotocolthat translates research results into deployment decisions. This
paper targets threecore gapsand threesecondary gaps:
• Core Gap A – Parameter selection without “stability regions”:many pipelines
still select a single optimum, whereas extreme points are typically sensitive and prone to
breakdown under small perturbations; there is a lack of policies that prioritizestable plateaus
and avoidcliff-type sensitivity regions.
• Core Gap B – WF A without leakage control for stateful strategies:na ¨ ıve train–test
splitting can yield optimistic evaluations forstateful/path-dependentstrategies [ 3], as indicator
overlap and position state may “bleed” across boundaries, reducing the “blindness” of the
forward test.
• Core Gap C – V alidation decoupled from execution and risk:many pipelines optimize
thesignalfirst, appending costs and guards as an afterthought [ 9], whereas operational failures
often originate from microstructure friction (spread widening, adverse fills) and ill-timed
leverage accumulation.
• Secondary Gap 1 – Absence of deployment gates:explicit pass/fail checklists for the
IS→WF A→OOS progression are rare.
• Secondary Gap 2 – Safeguards without ablation:ablation studies that disable individual
protection layers to measure their marginal contribution to tail-risk reduction are seldom
conducted.
•Secondary Gap 3 – Search transparency:descriptions ofdegrees of freedom(grid/trial
sizes) and parameter-locking policies are frequently omitted, impeding assessment of selection
bias and reproducibility.
1.2 Proposal and Contributions
We propose theAlgoXpert Alpha Research F ramework, an IS–WF A–OOS pipelineoriented
toward deployment decisionsthat aims to reduce overfitting and improve generalizability. Rather
than maximizing “peak backtest” metrics, the framework prioritizes robustness across multiple
validation stages. The main contributions are:
• (C1) Stability-region parameter selection:instead of selecting a single optimum, we
prioritize configurations within a “stable region” (plateau) and avoid sensitivity-prone “cliff”
zones. The IS stability region is defined as:
Ωstable ={θ|SR(θ)≥0.9·SR opt},
retaining configurations whose risk-adjusted Sharpe achieves at least 90% of the best observed
IS configuration. Details on the search space, α selection, and cliff-veto criteria are provided
in Section 3.2.
• (C2) Purged rolling walk-forward analysis:we design WF A with rolling windows and
insertpurge gapsbetween train and test segments to mitigate leakage and carryover effects,
which is particularly important forstateful/path-dependentstrategies (e.g., grid, trailing,
inventory state). The protocol description and WF A schedule are detailed in Section 3.2.
• (C3) Defense-in-depth safeguards:execution and equity constraints are embedded directly
into the pipeline (e.g., spread/leverage guards, circuit breakers, andkill switch). Addition-
ally, the framework mandatesstress testingby degrading execution assumptions (increasing
spread/commission or simulating adverse fills) to assess resilience. Details on metrics and
stress envelope are provided in Section 4.2.
• (C4) Decision gates and checklists:the framework provides pass/fail criteria for the
IS→WF A→OOS progression viadecision gatesthat arepre-committed. At Stage II, gates
2
are anchored toforward windows: each fold i is deemedPASSonly if the metric vector
on W test
i meets the minimum benchmarkb; the entire WF A isPASSif the proportion
of passing folds meets threshold q (majority-pass), andF AILimmediately upon triggering
thecatastrophic veto(e.g., drawdown integrity breakdown or constraint violation C). The
majority-pass + catastrophic-veto mechanism and fold-level evaluation procedure are described
directly in Algorithm 1.
Beyond gate criteria, we additionally report train→test degradation diagnostics (e.g., resilience
ratio) to illuminate failure modes; these diagnostics are not used for pass/fail decisions or
tuning.
1.3 Scope and Assumptions
The framework focuses on time-series trading strategies, particularly stateful/path-dependent
ones, where information leakage at train–test boundaries and carryover effects readily produce
optimistic evaluations. The procedure adheres to chronology with pre-committed decision gates;
train→test degradation diagnostics serve failure-mode analysis under a diagnostic-only principle
(see Section 3.4).
1.4 Paper Organization
Section 2 surveys related work. Section 3 presents the IS–WF A–OOS protocol, purge/state-
normalization mechanisms, and the safeguard architecture. Section 4 illustrates the framework
via a case study following chronology. Section 5 provides a post-validation report for research
and portfolio orientation. Section 6 discusses deployment implications, limitations, and future
directions.
2 Related Work
A strategy may achieve very high Sharpe or returns in backtesting solely becausetoo many
configurations, variants, or rules have been tested on the same dataset. As the number of trials
increases, the probability of a “lucky hit” (false discovery) also increases, even when no genuine
edge exists. Harvey et al. emphasize the risk of false discoveries in expected-return research
as the number of hypotheses (factors/strategies) proliferates, necessitating multiple-testing
controls [5]. From a practitioner’s perspective, Bailey et al. characterize backtest overfitting as a
natural consequence of R&D pipelines with large degrees of freedom and performance-based
strategy selection [ 4]. These results reinforce the argument that “peak backtest” is insufficient
for deployment decisions without a protocol that limits overfitting and selection bias.
Even when a researcher reports only asinglefinal strategy, results may still be biased
because that strategy was selected after extensive experimentation (data snooping). White
proposed theReality Checkto test the performance of “the best strategy in a candidate set”
after data-snooping, rather than testing each strategy independently [ 6]. Hansen subsequently
introduced theSuperior Predictive Ability (SPA)test to increase power and reduce sensitivity to
low-quality candidates in the comparison set [7]. These lines of work demonstrate that if one’s
pipeline generates multiple candidates, the evaluation must account for the selection process
rather than treating the result as a single-shot test.
A complementary strand adjusts popular metrics to avoid optimistic assessment. Bailey
and L´ opez de Prado proposed theDeflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR)to correct the observed Sharpe
for sample length, non-normality of returns, and selection bias from multiple trials [ 8]. The
pragmatic message is: the “raw” Sharpe can be substantially inflated when the strategy is
selected from a large search space; therefore one must either (i) reduce degrees of freedom, (ii)
employ bias-corrected metrics/tests, or combine both.

Figure 1: IS→WF A→OOS deployment decision protocol with decision gates.
WF A is a common practice in systematic strategy evaluation and is typically described as
rolling train–forward testing over time [ 1]. However, temporal splitting alone does not guarantee
elimination of all forms of leakage. For example, long indicator lookbacks cause overlap at
train/test boundaries; or strategies with position management (grid, trailing, inventory) make
current decisions dependent on the prior path. In such cases, the forward test may be less “blind”
than expected, producing optimistic assessments. Therefore, WF A is truly useful for deployment
decisions only when accompanied by leakage-control rules and explicit pass/fail gates, rather
than serving merely as a performance report.
In finance, especially when labels/features overlap temporally, L´ opez de Prado emphasizes
purgingandembargoto reduce information leakage between train and test [ 3]. The intuition
is to create a buffer around the cutoff point to mitigate serial correlation and overlap effects.
This paper inherits that principle and translates it into apurge gapwithin rolling WF A,
targeting both feature-level leakage (indicator overlap) and state-level leakage (state carryover)
for path-dependent strategies.
The gap between backtest and live performance often originates from execution friction:
spread widening, slippage, thin liquidity, and regime-dependent costs. The algorithmic trading
and optimal execution literature demonstrates that observed performance depends heavily on
cost/fill assumptions, necessitating stress testing under adverse scenarios (e.g., cost inflation,
degraded fill quality) to identify the strategy’s “breaking point” [ 9, 10]. From a risk-management
perspective, circuit breakers and kill switches are operational-level safeguards designed to limit
losses during tail events or when market conditions deviate from model assumptions; they
function as “emergency stops” rather than components of performance optimization.
In summary, the above research strands indicate that: (i) selection bias and multiple testing
must be controlled at the process level, (ii) WF A requires leakage-mitigation mechanisms for
stateful strategies, and (iii) evaluation must be execution-aware through stress testing and
operational safeguards.
In contrast to work that focuses on proposing new alphas, this paper focuses on a deployable
workflow: (i) parameter selection via stability regions rather than extreme points, (ii) purged
rolling WF A for stateful strategies, (iii) stress testing under execution assumptions with opera-
tional safeguards, and (iv) auditable decision gates and checklists. The goal is to reduce selection
bias, enhance reproducibility, and improve decision quality when transitioning from research to
operations.
3 Methods
3.1 Overview of the Deployment Decision Protocol (IS→WF A→OOS)
The goal of this work is to standardize adeployment decision protocolthat reduces
overfitting and selection bias in quantitative strategy R&D. The protocol enforceschronology
anddecision gatesof the pass/fail type: a strategy advances only upon passing validation in
strict chronological order.
As illustrated in Figure 1, the framework comprises three stages: (i)ISperforms stability
mapping to identify Ωstable (plateau) and discard cliff-sensitive configurations; (ii)purged rolling
4
WF Aevaluates sequential adaptability through rolling folds with purge gap g, accompanied by
diagnostics (e.g., ηi) and gate/veto mechanisms to lock the final configuration θ⋆; (iii)strict
OOS holdoutprovides the final validation underno further tuning. Pass/fail thresholds are
pre-committedbefore opening OOS.
In parallel with the three stages, the protocol integratesdefense-in-depth safeguards
and astress-test battery(execution/microstructure, equity protection), and defines ablation
∆M(g) to measure the marginal contribution of each safeguard.
Decision states.The protocol returns one of three outcomes:Deploy(all gates passed),
Reject(fail/veto), orRefactor(failure mode is structural in nature, requiring design changes
rather than parameter tuning).
Branching policy (Policy A).In this paper, we adoptPolicy Afor branching decisions:
G1 fail ⇒ Refactor(structural/design-level failure),G2 fail ⇒ Reject(insufficient sequential
adaptability), andG3 fail⇒Reject(failed strict holdout).
3.2 Strategy Class and Notation
Consider a stateful, path-dependent algorithmic trading strategy, applicable to position-management
paradigms such as trailing/grid/inventory. Market data are denoted D = {xt}T
t=1, where xt may
include bid/ask prices, spread, and other necessary microstructure variables. The strategy is
parameterized by a vectorθ∈Θ and generates a sequence of trading decisions over time.
State and action.At time t, the strategy maintains an internal state st (e.g., open positions,
grid level, trailing state, margin usage). Based on the observationx t and states t, the strategy
produces an action at ∈ A(open/close/adjust position, choose lot size, set/adjust stop/take-
profit), while simultaneously updating the state:
at =π θ(xt, st), s t+1 =f θ(st, xt, at),(1)
where πθ is the trading policy and fθ describes the state dynamics. Making st explicit is important
because, for path-dependent strategies, na ¨ ıve temporal train/test splitting can introduce leakage
at window boundaries (due to indicator/lookback overlap and position state “bleeding” across
boundaries), making the forward test less “blind” than expected. This directly motivates the
purged WF A design in Section 3.1.
Execution and risk constraints.Let C denote the set of execution/risk constraints (e.g.,
spread guard, leverage/DL limit, position count limit, circuit breakers/kill-switch). A parameter
configuration θ is considered feasible if, throughout the entire simulation, all generated actions
satisfy these constraints:
πθ feasible⇐ ⇒(xt, st, at)|=C ∀t.(2)
In our framework, C is enforced throughout IS/WF A/OOS and during stress tests, ensuring that
the validation process is execution-aware rather than purely signal-optimized.
Equity curve and risk measures.Let Et denote equity at time t. The running peak through
timetisE peak(t) = maxu≤t Eu. Equity-based drawdown at timetis defined as:
DDeq(t) = Epeak(t)−E t
Epeak(t) ,MDD eq = max
t
DDeq(t).(3)
Performance and robustness measures (e.g., Sharpe, Calmar, recovery factor, worst-window loss,
turnover/holding-time proxies) and their formal estimation/reporting procedures are presented
5
in Section 4.2. In the methods section, the above notation is used to describe the decision gates,
particularly the “drawdown integrity” criterion in the OOS stage and the equity-protection
layers in the safeguards.
3.3 Stage I: In-Sample Stability Mapping
The IS stage aims to produce a set ofrobustparameter candidates for WF A (Section 3.4),
rather than selecting a single backtest optimum [ 4]. Extreme points are typically sensitive to
data noise, regime changes, and minor execution-assumption discrepancies; accordingly, the
framework prioritizes “plateaus” (near-optimal but stable regions) and discards “cliffs” (sensitive
zones where small parameter perturbations can cause performance collapse or drawdown spikes).
3.3.1 Stability RegionΩ stable (Plateau Selection)
On the IS dataset, we perform a performance mapping over a finite search space (grid or trials
with budget B) to observe the topology of SR(θ), while simultaneously tracking minimum
tail-risk indicators (particularly MDD eq(θ)). Let
SRopt = max
θ∈Θ
SR(θ) (4)
denote the bestobservedvalue in IS under budget B (not to be interpreted as the true optimum).
Viability condition.Stage I is considered viable only if
SRopt ≥SR min >0.(5)
If this condition is not met, the strategy terminates with verdictRefactor(Policy A), rather
than proceeding to plateau selection.
The stability region is defined as:
Ωstable ={θ∈Θ|SR(θ)≥α·SR opt},(6)
where α∈ (0, 1) is the plateau threshold (default α = 0.9; sensitivity analysis with respect to α
is reported in Section 4.4). Configurations outside Ω stable are excluded due to markedly inferior
performance or the risk of spurious selection under large degrees of freedom.
T rade-count feasibility filter.To avoid configurations with high SR but based on too few
observations (unreliable and potentially lucky), we apply a minimum trade-count threshold in
IS:
Ntrades(θ)≥N min.(7)
Configurations failing this threshold are removed from Ω stable before shortlisting.
Ranking withinΩ stable.After plateau and feasibility filtering, ranking/shortlisting is per-
formedwithinΩ stable. Rather than maximizing SR (which risks reverting to extreme-point
selection), we prioritize criteria related to tail-risk and recovery (e.g., Calmar, recovery factor,
worst-window loss), aiming to select candidates with reasonable risk characteristics before
advancing to WF A.
3.3.2 Cliff Filtering (Sensitivity V eto)
The objective of cliff filtering is to remove configurations that are “good but thin”: a very
small parameter change causes sharp performance degradation or drawdown spikes. For each θ,
consider the neighborhood N (θ) consisting of adjacent points along each parameter dimension
with displacement equal to one grid resolution step (e.g., ±1 step), holding other dimensions
fixed.
6
Performance cliff (Sharpe).
Cliff SR(θ) = max
θ′∈N(θ)

SR(θ)−SR(θ ′)


where [z]+ = max(z, 0) measures the worst-case Sharpe degradation under minimal perturbation.
T ail-risk cliff (equity drawdown).
Cliff DD(θ) = max
θ′∈N(θ)

MDDeq(θ′)−MDD eq(θ)

+ .(9)
Rejection rule.A configuration is rejected if it is sensitive under either criterion:
θis rejected if Cliff SR(θ)> τSR or Cliff DD(θ)> τDD.(10)
In practice, cliff filtering is appliedwithinΩ stable to favor plateau regions with “thickness”
(robustness) over thin peaks (fragility).
3.3.3 Parameter Locking Policy
After Stage I, the framework applies aparameter lockingpolicy to constrain degrees of
freedom and reduce selection bias across subsequent time windows:
• Lock time:locking occurs after completing plateau selection, feasibility filtering, and cliff
veto, simultaneously finalizing a finite shortlist.
• Locked dimensions:cliff-prone parameter dimensions or those governing high-risk behavior
(e.g., core parameters or those increasing position/leverage accumulation) are fixed, or restricted
to a very small pre-reported subset.
• WF A degrees of freedom:during WF A, each fold may only re-select θi from the shortlist
(or the locked subset), without re-opening the entire Θ or altering the strategy structure.
The shortlist size, search budget B, α value, threshold Nmin, and cliff thresholds ( τSR, τDD)
must be reported to allow readers to assess the degrees of freedom, selection bias, and repro-
ducibility of the pipeline.
3.4 Stage II: Purged Rolling Walk-Forward Analysis
The WF A stage [1] is designed to test thesequential adaptabilityof the strategy: an alpha is
considereddeployableonly if it maintains quality when shifted through time. Forstateful/path-
dependentstrategies (grid/trailing/inventory), na ¨ ıve train/test splitting can produce optimistic
evaluations due to (i) lookback overlap at window boundaries, and (ii)state carryover[ 3].
Accordingly, we employpurged rolling WF Aand impose pass/faildecision gatesbefore
opening OOS.
Procedure summary .Algorithm 1 describes the WF A in pseudocode, emphasizing restricted
DoF, purge, state normalization, majority-pass gate, and catastrophic veto.
3.4.1 Rolling F old Structure and Purge Gapg
The WF A period is divided into N folds according to a fixed, pre-defined schedule. Each fold i
consists of a training window, followed by a purge gap, and then an out-of-sample test window:
W train
i →purge gapg→W test
i .(11)
The purge gap g is a time segment excluded immediately after W train
i to mitigate overlap/serial-
dependence effects between train and test (especially when features have long lookbacks), and
to reduce leakage risk from stateful strategies. For intraday data, g is expressed in trading days
or bars and isfixedacross all folds to avoid result-dependent tuning.

Note on rolling schedule and purge/train overlap between folds.In a consecutive
rolling design, the purge gap of the preceding fold may overlap with the beginning of W train
i+1 .
Thisdoes not compromisethe “blindness” of the forward test, since the critical condition to
maintain is:each W test
i is always separated fromits own W train
i by a purge gap immediately
preceding it. In other words, the purge is defined by theintra-fold train–test relationship, not by
the inter-fold distance.
3.4.2 State Normalization Prior to F orward T esting
To control leakage fromstate carryoverin path-dependent strategies, the forward test of each
fold is evaluated under anormalized stateassumption at the start of W test
i . Specifically, at
the test-window boundary, the strategy is reset to a canonical state (e.g.,flat/no inventory, grid
level = 0, trailing state reset, all internal counters/state variables reinitialized).
In terms of implementation, state normalization can be realized in one of two equivalent
ways: (i) run the W test
i simulation as an independent session starting exactly at the test-window
opening; or (ii) force-close all positions and reinitialize internal state variables immediately
before entering W test
i . The objective is to ensure that performance within W test
i reflects temporal
generalizability, rather than benefiting from accumulated state in prior segments.
8
3.4.3 Re-Optimization Rule and Degrees-of-F reedom Constraints
Within each fold i, configuration selection is permitted only within the controlled scope from
Stage I:
θi ∈Ω stable (or a locked shortlist from Stage I).(12)
This rule ensures WF A does not degenerate into unconstrained optimization across multiple
windows (which would inflate selection bias). Per the protocol, WF A isnotpermitted to (i)
re-open the entire parameter space Θ, or (ii) alter the strategy structure. All permitted “degrees
of freedom” (e.g., selection restricted to a shortlist of size K; or only a subset of execution
parameters may be adjusted) must be stated explicitly and held fixed across all folds.
Upon completing WF A, a final configuration θ⋆ is selected according to apre-committed
rule, for example: the configuration with the best median- SRtest,i across valid folds, or selection
based on risk criteria (Calmar/MDD) across test windows. Crucially, the rule for selecting θ⋆
must be finalized before opening OOS.
3.4.4 Per-F old F orward Metrics and Gate Design Principles
For each foldi, denote the Sharpe ratio on train and forward test:
SRtrain,i :=SR(π θi;W train
i ), SR test,i :=SR(π θi;W test
i ),(13)
where SR(·) is defined and reported in detail in Section 4.2. On the forward window, we
simultaneously evaluate risk/feasibility metrics (e.g., MDDeq,test,i, Calmartest,i, trade density,
and the degree ofCviolation).
Principles. Decision gates are anchored to forward windows.Rationale: W test
i con-
stitutes the temporally “blind” test, and therefore the WF A pass/fail criteria are constructed
primarily from metrics on W test
i (benchmark-based).T rain metrics serve only operational
and diagnostic roles.Metrics on W train
i primarily support θi selection within restricted DoF;
if needed, train →test indicators may be reported as diagnostics, but they do not constitute a
tuning loop and are not primary gate conditions.
3.4.5 WF A Decision Gate: Majority-Pass + Catastrophic V eto (Pre-committed)
Letbdenote the minimum benchmark vector on the forward window (e.g., comprising Sharpe,
drawdown, Calmar thresholds, and trade-density conditions), and q∈ (0, 1] the minimum
proportion of folds that must meet the benchmark (pre-committed). A fold i is deemedP ASS
on the forward window if:
Mtest,i ⪰b,(14)
whereM test,i is the metric vector computed onW test
i , and⪰denotes “meets all thresholds.”
(1) Majority-pass persistence.Let I denote the set ofevaluablefolds (no data/simulation
failures and meeting minimum evaluation conditions). The requirement is:

I


≥q.(15)
(2) Catastrophic veto.If any W test
i exhibits a catastrophic violation according to the pre-
committed veto rule (e.g., drawdown exceeding the integrity threshold, or kill-switch/leverage-cap
violation perC), the entire WF AF AILs immediately.
9
(3) OOS is not opened upon WF A F AIL.Following the decision-oriented principle, if
WF AF AILs, the strategy does not proceed to OOS holdout (verdict:RejectorRefactor
depending on failure mode). If WF APASSes, the final parameters θ⋆ arelockedand the
strategy advances to Stage III underno further tuning.
Train→test diagnostics (not used for gating).Beyond gate criteria, we additionally
report ∆SRi := SRtest,i −SR train,i and resilience ratio ηi := SRtest,i/SRtrain,i for failure-mode
analysis.Overarching principle:diagnostics serve only post-hoc analysis and arenotused as
gate conditions or for tuningθ ⋆.
4 Empirical Study
4.1 Experimental Design
The objective of the experimental design is tooperationalizethe IS →WF A→OOS protocol
described in Section 3 on a minimal but auditable case study that respectschronology(no
peeking) and introducesno additional degrees of freedombeyond what the protocol permits.
4.1.1 Data, Market, and Execution Assumptions
Market and frequency.The case study uses the USDJPY pair on the M5 timeframe.
Backtests are run using theEvery Ticksimulation mode of MetaTrader 5, reflecting the tester’s
tick-driven order-matching mechanism.
Data source and time span.Data are sourced from broker Exness; the backtest period
covers 01/01/2022–31/12/2025. Throughout the experiment, data are used in chronological
order, and all decisions/estimates at each stage rely only on the data permitted for observation
at that stage.
Capital and leverage assumptions.The initial deposit is 100,000 with nominal lever-
age of 1:100. These settings are held fixed throughout IS/WF A/OOS to avoid confounding
environmental changes with alpha quality changes.
Execution model and transaction costs.The baseline setup uses anideal execution
model per the tester (no latency simulation; no adverse slippage beyond the default tick-driven
mechanism). Spread/commission assumptions (if separately configured) are considered part of
the backtest environment and must be documented under “Backtest Assumptions” to ensure
reproducibility and proper inference scope. Results in the experimental section should therefore
be interpreted as abaseline under simplified execution.
Execution-awareness and stress envelope (reporting scope).Per the logic of Gap C, a
complete deployment evaluation requires astress-test battery(e.g., increased spread/commission,
simulated adverse fills). However, in the current reporting version, the team hasnotconducted
stress branches due to resource constraints; instead, we describe the stress envelope as a
mandatory framework templateand defer stress/ablation results to future versions (see
discussion in Section 6).
4.1.2 IS/WF A/OOS Splits and WF A Schedule (Rolling + Purge)
Time-ordered splits (no peeking).The timeline is partitioned in strict chronological order
to reflect deployment conditions:
•In-Sample (IS):01/01/2022–31/12/2023,

permitted only within the controlled scope from Stage I (restricted DoF; shortlist/Ω stable), and

stated in Section 3.4.

4.3 Experimental Results
4.3.1 Stage I (IS): Benchmark Check and Viability
Over the IS period (01/01/2022–31/12/2023), the strategy achieved: Sharpe = 2 .12, Calmar
≈ 1.69, MaxDD (equity) = 6 .46%, N = 2625 trades. Per the benchmark vectorb(Section 4.2),
the Sharpe/Calmar/MaxDD thresholds are met. The trade-density criterion (trades/day) has
not been directly reported; this is noted as areporting gapto be addressed.
4.3.2 Stage II (WF A): F orward-Window Results
Table 2 summarizes metrics on W test
i and gate status per pre-committed benchmarks. Train
metrics serve internal audit only (diagnostic-only principle, Section 3.4).
Aggregate WF A (mean across forward segments): Sharpe = 3 .79, MaxDD = 2 .93%, Calmar
= 7.54.
Gate verdict.Step 2F AIL(Sharpe < 2.0), Step 1 and Step 3PASS. With q = 2/3, WF A
concludesPASS; no fold triggers catastrophic veto (MDD<7%).
Audit note.SR on W train
1 is strongly negative while W test
1 is high, possibly due to state
normalization or train/test simulation-configuration differences. Per the diagnostic-only principle
(Section 3.4), train metrics do not affect the verdict.
4.3.3 Stage III (OOS): One-Y ear Holdout and V erdict
On the OOS holdout (01/01/2025–31/12/2025) with θ⋆ locked (Section 3.4), the strategy
achieved: Sharpe = 2 .34, Calmar = 3 .01, MaxDD = 4 .21%, N = 1374 trades. Perb:
Sharpe/Calmar/MaxDD pass. The trades/day criterion requires further cross-referencing.
4.4 Results Analysis
4.4.1 V erdict Summary by Decision Gates (IS→WF A→OOS)
Per the protocol in Section 3.1, decisions are anchored topre-committeddecision gates. Under
the current benchmarks (Sharpe ≥ 2.0, Calmar ≥ 1.5, MaxDD < 7%, and minimum trade
density), the case study shows: (i) Stage I (IS) meets performance thresholds with sufficient
13
trade count, (ii) Stage II (WF A) achieves majority-pass on forward windows despite one fold
failing on Sharpe, and (iii) Stage III (OOS) recovers benchmark compliance under strict holdout
(no tuning). Therefore,within the current simulation assumptions, the alpha may be regarded
asstatistically gate-compliant through time. However, a “deploy” conclusion is valid only
when execution assumptions are further verified via the stress envelope (see Section 4.4.5).
4.4.2 Stage I (IS): Viability and Sample Thickness
IS results (SR = 2 .12, Calmar ≈ 1.69, MaxDD = 6 .46%, N = 2625 trades) demonstrate that the
strategy passes the minimum viability condition and is not based on a handful of observations
(a common backtest-overfitting failure mode). In the framework’s logic (Section 3.3), this is a
necessary condition for advancing from “alpha idea” to “testable candidate.”
An important interpretive note: IS here is used tolock the candidate space(plateau/shortlist)
rather than to demonstrate generalizability. Accordingly, in the experimental section, IS should
be read as evidence that the strategyhas a sufficiently strong signalunder the stated cost/fill
model, not as deployment evidence.
4.4.3 Stage II (WF A): Sequential Adaptability and Regime Heterogeneity
WF A forward results show high average performance (SR = 3 .79, MaxDD = 2 .93%, Calmar
= 7.54), but fold-level decomposition reveals heterogeneity: Step 2 has forward SR = 1 .36 (below
the Sharpe threshold), while Step 1 and Step 3 achieve SR = 3 .81 and = 6 .20, respectively.
Under the majority-pass design (Section 3.4), this case is interpreted as follows:
• The signal is not absolutely stable across all time segments,but exhibitsmajority-
window robustness(2/3 folds pass). This represents “meeting the minimum threshold for
advancement” rather than “perfect stability.”
• Step 2 serves as a regime-sensitivity indicator:this fold should be viewed as a
naturalstress-by-time(not cost stress), suggesting that the alpha may degrade under certain
volatility/microstructure regimes.
• No catastrophic veto:all forward windows maintain MaxDD below 7%, hence no integrity-
constraint veto is triggered. This reinforces the framework’s argument thattail-risk control
must be prioritized alongside performance.
Audit flag: negative train SR at Step 1.As noted in Section 4.3, strongly negative SR
on W train
1 (−4.81) while W test
1 is high warrants audit: (i) re-optimization mechanism, (ii) state
normalization at the test boundary, and (iii) consistency in Sharpe definition between train/test.
Recommendation: reportfold-level forward metrics + gateas primary results; train metrics
are for audit purposes.
4.4.4 Stage III (OOS): Strict Holdout and Reasonable Degradation
OOS results (SR = 2.34, Calmar = 3.01, MaxDD = 4.21%, N = 1374 trades) meet the benchmark
under strict no-tuning holdout, consistent with the selection-bias reduction objective.
Relative comparison through time:
• OOS SR vs. IS:2 .34 vs. 2 .12 shows no degradation, suggesting that performance is not
merely a peak IS optimum.
• OOS SR vs. WF A (forward mean):2 .34 is lower than 3 .79, indicatingperformance
normalizationupon entering the holdout. This is a commonly observed pattern consistent
with the “not maximizing peak backtest” thesis.
• Drawdown integrity:OOS MaxDD (4 .21%) falls between IS (6 .46%) and WF A (2.93%),
implying that risk does not spike upon entering the holdout.
14
Overall, OOS results provide the strongest evidence in this paper forgeneralization under
chronology, but must still be placed in the context of execution assumptions (see below).
4.4.5 Execution Limitations and Missing Stress Envelope
All current results are produced underideal executionassumptions (no latency, no adverse
slippage beyond the MT5 tick model). Since the paper highlights Gap C, the absence of a stress
envelope implies:
•Time-robustness under the current cost model can be established,
• butexecution-robustness under microstructure/cost inflation cannot yet be con-
cluded.
In the absence of time to re-run multiple batteries, the scientifically appropriate presentation
is: (i) explicitly state this asbaseline validationwith stress-testing asplanned work,
(ii) retain the stress protocol as atemplate(without presenting results), (iii) add ascope
limitationstatement at the end of the experimental section: “deployment-readiness requires
passing a cost/slippage stress envelope; results herein should be interpreted under baseline MT5
assumptions.”
4.4.6 Implications for F uture Alpha Evaluation and Ranking
The WF A/OOS portion of the case study should be understood as asingle validation unit
foronealpha. For future multi-alpha leaderboards, the framework suggests a final ranking
principle that ispost-validationin nature: rank only alphas that havePASSed IS →WF A→OOS
(and eventually the stress envelope), then compare using a pre-committed utility (e.g., prioritize
MaxDD/Calmar over SR when the mandate iscapital preservation). This clearly separates the
deployment decision protocolfrom theresearch portfolio report(portfolio/roadmap),
consistent with the spirit of the introduction.
5 Post-Validation Comparison of Alpha Variants
This section constitutes apost-validation report,nota step within the framework’s decision
gates. The objective is to compare four alpha variants that havepassed the IS →WF A→OOS
pipeline under the same data setup and execution assumptions described in Section 4.1, serving
research orientation and capital allocation (portfolio view).
5.1 Comparison Protocol
Four candidates (v1–v4) are evaluated according to three principles:
1.OOS-first ranking:primary ranking is based on OOS holdout (Sharpe, Calmar, MDD eq),
as this constitutes the strictest “blind” test [1, 4].
2. WF A dispersion: SRWF A and inter-fold range measure stability across market regimes
(diagnostic, not used for ranking).
3. Diagnostic-only:train metrics and resilience ratio ηi serve analysis only; they are not used
to modify rankings or parameters afterθ ⋆ has been locked (Section 3.4).

Table 4 presentsSR test,i across each forward fold along with the mean and range.
All four alphas are relatively weak in Fold 2 ( SR≈ 1.1–1.4) and strong in Fold 3 (SR≈ 5–6),
indicating a common regime dependence—this is precisely why rolling WF A is needed rather
than relying on a single backtest (see Section 3.4).
5.4 Equity-Path Diagnostics (OOS)
Figure 2 enables visual inspection of failure modes that aggregate statistics may obscure: (i) profit
concentrated in a short segment, (ii) extended plateaus, or (iii) slow recovery after drawdown.
5.5 Trade-Off and Mandate-Dependent Recommendations
Ranking depends on the objective function:
• Maximizing risk-adjusted return: v3 (Sharpe) or v2 (Calmar) are the leading candidates.
• Capital preservation (tail-risk): v4 has the lowest MDDOOS; the trade-off is lower
Sharpe/Calmar compared tov2/v3.
The rank reversal when switching from maximizing Sharpe to minimizing MaxDD reflects the
fundamental trade-off between performance and tail-risk control [ 8], underscoring thatno single
metric sufficesfor alpha ranking—a multi-dimensional criterion set tied to a specific mandate is
required.
16
Figure 2: Normalized OOS equity (v1–v4).
5.6 Limitations of the Post-Validation Report
• Results are based onideal executionassumptions (Section 4.1.1); rankings may change under
a cost/slippage stress envelope.
• Comparing four variants from the same search process increases degrees of freedom at the
research level; interpreting small differences between v2 and v3 requires supplementary tests
(Deflated Sharpe [8], SPA [7], or HAC confidence intervals [11]).
6 Discussion and Conclusion
6.1 Practical Implications for Deployment and Governance
This paper treatsdeploymentas an auditable decision rather than a backtest optimization
outcome. Accordingly, the primary “deliverable” is not a single alpha but astandardized
validation protocolthat produces aPASS/F AILverdict following IS→WF A→OOS chronology
andpre-committeddecision gates.
Minimum deployment checklist.A strategy is deemed eligible for advancement only if it
simultaneously satisfies:
• Chronology & no-peeking: θ⋆ locked after WF A; no tuning upon opening OOS (Section 3.4).
• Restricted DoF:WF A re-selects within Ω stable/shortlist; Θ is not re-opened after Stage I
(Section 3.3).
• Gate on forward windows:majority-pass + catastrophic veto (Section 3.4, Algorithm 1).
• Execution-aware reporting:execution assumptions are explicitly declared; results are
interpreted within that context rather than defaulting to “deployable.”
Governance/audit: requirements for auditable research.To reduce selection bias and
enhance reproducibility, research should be accompanied by a minimum “evidence pack”:
17
• Search transparency:description of search budget, grid/trial size, permitted parameter
dimensions, and locking timing.
• Artifact logging:backtest configuration, data version, seed (if applicable), and stage-by-stage
results (IS/WF A/OOS) under a common metric standard.
• Decision trace:explicit pass/fail rules (benchmarkb, majority threshold q, catastrophic
veto) and failure-mode-to-verdict mapping (Rejectvs.Refactor).
These requirements separate “attractive results” from “valid process,” making conclusions
falsifiable: if OOS is opened and the gate fails, the strategy is eliminated without “rescue
tuning.”
6.2 Limitations
Results should be interpreted in the context of the following limitations (see also Section 4.4.5):
• Simplified execution:ideal execution assumed; latency/slippage not explicitly modeled—
increases optimism risk for microstructure-sensitive strategies.
• Single asset/broker:USDJPY M5 case study on MT5/Exness; transferability has not been
verified.
• Incomplete stress/ablation:stress envelope described as a template; current results
constitute baseline validation.
• Multi-alpha selection bias:comparing v1–v4 increases DoF at the research level; small
differences require supplementary testing [8, 7].
6.3 Future Directions
Direct extensions to enhance deployability and scientific rigor include:
• Multi-asset & multi-timescale validation:replicate the protocol across multiple FX
pairs/assets and timeframes; report regime-conditional stability (trend/range, volatility
regimes).
• More realistic execution models:incorporate explicit latency/slippage models, cost-ladder
stress envelopes (spread/commission/slippage inflation), and “point-of-failure” testing.
• Portfolio-level governance:transition from single-alpha evaluation to portfolio selec-
tion/retention/exit processes (correlation, tail co-movement, risk budgeting), with post-
deployment monitoring rules.
• Pre-registration and selection-bias testing:pre-commit benchmarks, number of trials,
and alpha selection criteria; apply appropriate tests/corrections for multiple hypothesis testing.
• Live paper trading:add a paper/live-sim stage as a “deployment rehearsal” to verify
microstructure assumptions before real capital allocation.
6.4 Conclusion
This paper proposes theAlgoXpert Alpha Research Framework, a decision-oriented IS–
WF A–OOS protocol designed to reduce overfitting and improve robustness when transitioning
from backtest to operations. The core contributions of the framework are (i) parameter selection
viastability regionsrather than extreme points, (ii)purged rolling WF Ato mitigate leakage
for stateful/path-dependent strategies, (iii) integrateddefense-in-depth safeguardsfollowing
an execution-aware approach, and (iv)auditable pass/fail decision gates.
Within the current illustrative scope, the framework isvalidatedin the sense that the
chronological validation procedure produces consistent and traceable decisions, while surfacing
failure modes requiring audit (e.g., train–test discrepancies by fold) rather than concealing them
behind aggregate statistics. Conversely, the frameworkshould notbe interpreted as a profit
guarantee; simulation assumptions (particularly latency/slippage) and the completeness of stress
testing/ablation are boundary conditions for deployability inference.
18
In summary, the primary value of the framework lies in standardizing thedeployment
decision processrather than maximizing “peak backtest”: an alpha advances only upon
passing pre-committed decision gates, under declared execution assumptions, with degrees of
freedom controlled to reduce selection bias.
